questions.question: Which of these can be named **only** with a `type` alias, never with an `interface`?
options: A union like `string | number`
An object shape with a `readonly` property
A callable object via a call signature
An object that extends another object type
questions.explanation: An `interface` can only describe an object (or callable/constructable object) type; only a `type` alias can name a union, tuple, primitive, mapped, conditional, or template-literal type. Object shapes, readonly members, call signatures, and extending are all expressible with interfaces.

questions.question: Declaring two `type` aliases with the same name in the same scope merges them into one alias, the same way two same-name interfaces merge.
questions.explanation: Only interfaces participate in declaration merging. Redeclaring a `type` alias with the same name is a `Duplicate identifier` error; same-name interfaces merge their members into one.

questions.question: Given `interface Config { readonly tags: string[]; }` and `const c: Config = { tags: ["a"] };`, which statement is true?
questions.code:
interface Config { readonly tags: string[]; }
const c: Config = { tags: ["a"] };
options: `c.tags.push("b")` is allowed, but `c.tags = []` is a compile error
Both `c.tags.push("b")` and `c.tags = []` are compile errors
Both `c.tags.push("b")` and `c.tags = []` are allowed
`c.tags.push("b")` is a compile error, but `c.tags = []` is allowed
questions.explanation: `readonly` is shallow: it blocks reassigning the property binding (`c.tags = []` fails) but does not make the referenced array immutable, so `c.tags.push("b")` is permitted.

questions.question: A `Point3D` value (with `x`, `y`, `z`) can be assigned to a `Point2D` variable (requiring only `x`, `y`), because a wider object is a subtype of a narrower one.
questions.explanation: Width subtyping: an object with more properties is a subtype of one with fewer required members, so the wider shape is assignable where the narrower is expected (the extra `z` is ignored for a non-fresh value). The reverse fails because the required `z` would be missing.

questions.question: Two interfaces `Dog` and `Robot` each declare exactly `legs: number` and `bark(): void`. A `Dog` value is assigned to a `Robot`-typed variable. What happens?
options: It compiles â€” TypeScript is structural, so identical shapes are mutually assignable regardless of name
It fails â€” the interfaces have different names, which makes them distinct nominal types
It fails â€” assignment between unrelated named interfaces requires an explicit `implements`
It compiles only if `Robot` is declared with an explicit `extends Dog` clause
questions.explanation: TypeScript uses structural typing: compatibility is decided by shape, not name. Two structurally identical interfaces with different names are mutually assignable; no `implements` or `extends` relationship is needed.

questions.question: How does method-syntax `deposit(amount: number): void` differ from property-with-function-type `onChange: (balance: number) => void` in how their parameters are checked?
options: The method form is checked with parameter bivariance; the arrow-property form is checked contravariantly under `strictFunctionTypes`
The method form is checked contravariantly; the arrow-property form is checked with parameter bivariance
Both are always checked bivariantly regardless of `strictFunctionTypes`
Both are always checked contravariantly when `strict` is on
questions.explanation: Method-syntax members are checked with parameter bivariance (an unsound but convenient legacy behavior), whereas an arrow-typed property is checked contravariantly once `strictFunctionTypes` is enabled. This is why the two forms are 'almost' the same but not identical.

questions.question: Why does declaring `name: string` alongside `[key: string]: number` in the same interface produce an error?
options: A string index signature constrains every named property, and `string` is not assignable to the index value type `number`
An interface may not contain both a named property and an index signature at the same time
Index signatures must be declared after all named properties, not before them
`name` collides with a reserved key that index signatures implicitly reserve
questions.explanation: A string index signature requires every declared property's type to be assignable to its value type. Here `name: string` is not assignable to `number`, so it errors. Mixing named properties with an index signature is otherwise perfectly legal.

questions.question: With `type Permissions = Record<"admin" | "user" | "guest", boolean>`, how is reading `perms.admin` typed, and what happens for `perms.gues`?
options: `perms.admin` is `boolean`; `perms.gues` is a compile error because the key set is closed
`perms.admin` is `boolean | undefined`; `perms.gues` is `boolean | undefined`
`perms.admin` is `boolean | undefined`; `perms.gues` is a compile error
`perms.admin` is `boolean`; `perms.gues` is also `boolean` since all values share the type
questions.explanation: `Record<K, V>` enumerates an exact, closed key set, so `perms.admin` is simply `boolean` (no `undefined`), and a misspelled key like `gues` does not exist on the type and is a compile error. An open index signature would behave differently.

questions.question: Given `interface Dict { [key: string]: number; }` and `const d: Dict = { a: 1 };`, what is the type of `d["zzz"]` with `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` **enabled**, and what is the runtime value?
options: Type `number | undefined`; runtime value `undefined`
Type `number`; runtime value `undefined`
Type `number | undefined`; runtime value `0`
Type `never`; runtime value `undefined`
questions.explanation: `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` adds `undefined` to values read through an index signature, so the static type becomes `number | undefined`, matching the actual runtime value `undefined`. Without the flag the type would be a misleading `number`.

questions.question: `interface A { kind: string }` and `interface B { kind: number }`. How do `interface C extends A, B {}` and `type D = A & B` differ on the conflicting `kind`?
options: `C` errors at its declaration; `D` declares fine but `D["kind"]` is `never`
Both error at their declaration with the same message
Neither errors; both resolve `kind` to `string | number`
`C` resolves `kind` to `never`; `D` errors at its declaration
questions.explanation: `extends` is checked eagerly and errors at the declaration on an incompatible inherited member. `&` is lazy: it intersects `kind` to `string & number`, which collapses to `never`, so the type declares fine and the impossibility only surfaces when you try to build a value.

questions.question: Given `interface Options { width?: number; color: string; }`, which of these make `{ color: "red", widht: 10 }` (note the typo) compile against `Options`? Select all that apply.
options: Assigning the literal to a variable first, then assigning that variable to an `Options` target
Using a type assertion: `{ color: "red", widht: 10 } as Options`
Adding `[extra: string]: unknown` to the `Options` type
Passing the literal directly as a fresh argument to a function taking `Options`
Renaming `width?` to a required `width: number`
questions.explanation: Excess property checks fire only on fresh literals passed/assigned directly. Losing freshness via an intermediate variable, silencing with `as`, or adding a matching index signature all permit the extra key. Passing the literal directly still triggers the check, and making `width` required changes nothing about the unknown `widht` key.

questions.question: For a weak type like `interface Prefs { theme?: string; lang?: string; }` (all-optional members), assigning `{ notAPref: true }` is rejected because the source shares no property with `Prefs`.
questions.explanation: A weak type (all properties optional) gets an additional compatibility check that rejects a source object with zero overlapping properties, catching mistakes that pure width subtyping would otherwise allow.

questions.question: With `interface HasName { name: string }` and `interface MaybeName { name?: string }`, which assignment direction compiles?
options: `const a: MaybeName = must` (where `must: HasName`) compiles; `const b: HasName = maybe` (where `maybe: MaybeName`) does not
`const b: HasName = maybe` compiles; `const a: MaybeName = must` does not
Both directions compile because the property name matches
Neither direction compiles because the modifiers differ
questions.explanation: A required source property satisfies an optional target (required â†’ optional is fine). The reverse fails: an optional source `name?` may be `undefined`, which cannot satisfy a required target `name: string`.

questions.question: What is the type of `K`?
options: `never`, because `"x" & "y"` has no common value
`"x" | "y"`, because intersection unions the literal members
`"x"`, because the first interface wins the conflict
`string`, because both literals widen to their base type
questions.explanation: `AB["kind"]` is `"x" & "y"`; two distinct string literals share no value, so the intersection reduces to `never`. This is exactly why you model mutually exclusive alternatives with a union, not an intersection.

questions.question: `interface ReadName { readonly name: string }`, `interface MutName { name: string }`. Considering `const x: MutName = r` (where `r: ReadName`) and `const y: ReadName = m` (where `m: MutName`), which is correct?
options: Both compile â€” `readonly` is ignored for assignability, which is a known, intentional unsoundness
Only `const y: ReadName = m` compiles; mutable-to-readonly is the only safe direction
Only `const x: MutName = r` compiles; readonly-to-mutable is the only safe direction
Neither compiles â€” `readonly`-ness must match exactly for assignment
questions.explanation: `readonly`-ness is not considered during assignability in either direction, so both compile. This is a deliberate unsoundness: after `const x: MutName = r` you could mutate via `x.name`, defeating the read-only intent of `r`. `readonly` only blocks reassignment through the binding that declares it.

questions.question: When two same-name interfaces each declare a method `f` with different signatures, what is the result of the merge?
options: The two method declarations merge into overloads
It is an error, because non-compatible members cannot merge
The later declaration's `f` replaces the earlier one
Their parameter types are intersected into a single signature
questions.explanation: Merged function members (methods and call signatures) become overloads rather than conflicting. The 'conflict is an error' rule applies to non-function members; functions are combined into an overload set.

questions.question: Which statements about freshness and excess property checks are correct? Select all that apply.
options: Excess property checks apply only to fresh object literals assigned or passed directly to a typed target
The check fires recursively into nested fresh literals and through array literals of fresh objects
A matching index signature on the target legitimizes otherwise-excess keys
Once a literal is stored in a variable, the excess property check still applies when that variable is passed
Width subtyping, not the excess property check, governs a non-fresh value
questions.explanation: Excess property checks target fresh literals only, fire recursively into nested/array literals, and are satisfied by a matching index signature; non-fresh values fall back to width subtyping. Storing a literal in a variable loses freshness, so the check no longer applies when that variable is passed â€” making the fourth option false.

questions.question: Which statement about an `interface` extending a `type` alias is correct?
options: An interface can `extends` a type alias only if that alias resolves to an object shape, not a union
An interface can never `extends` a type alias; only another interface
An interface can `extends` any type alias, including a union, by distributing over its members
An interface can `extends` a type alias only if the alias was declared with `&`
questions.explanation: An interface may extend a type alias when the alias resolves to an object shape, but it cannot extend a union alias (`extends` builds object/interface types only). The `&` mechanism, by contrast, can intersect anything including unions.
